title: Panic Attacks: What Is Happening and What Helps
subtitle: A reading on the physiology of panic and what the evidence shows about managing it
excerpt: A reading on what is actually happening in the body during a panic attack, how the misinterpretation of those sensations makes it worse, and what the evidence shows about what helps.
sourceNotes: Draws on published cognitive and physiological research on panic disorder, including David Clark's cognitive model of panic and research on the fight-or-flight response. Framing is original to homemade.education. David Clark, 'A cognitive approach to panic', Behaviour Research and Therapy (1986). Foundational paper on the misinterpretation model of panic.

content.text: A panic attack is a rapid activation of the body's threat response when no actual threat is present. The symptoms include racing heart, shortness of breath, dizziness, and a strong sense that something is wrong. These are the sensations the body produces when it believes danger is real. Understanding what is happening does not stop it immediately. But it changes what the experience means. That shift is most of what drives the cycle.
What is happening in the body

The fight-or-flight response is designed to prepare the body for rapid physical action. Adrenaline and cortisol are released. Heart rate increases to pump more blood to the muscles. Breathing quickens to take in more oxygen. Blood is redirected away from digestion and toward the limbs. The result is a collection of strong physical sensations that are perfectly calibrated for outrunning a physical threat, and deeply alarming when there is no visible threat to outrun.
The cycle that makes it worse

Cognitive research on panic points to a specific mechanism: the misinterpretation of bodily sensations. When the heart rate increases, the person interprets this as a sign something is physically wrong. A heart attack. A breakdown. The start of something catastrophic. That interpretation is itself alarming. It drives the threat response higher. The physical sensations intensify. The interpretation seems confirmed. The panic feeds itself.

This is why understanding the physiology matters. "My heart is racing because my body thinks it is in danger" is a different interpretation from "my heart is racing, something is wrong with my heart." The first interpretation is accurate and has a known arc. The second drives the spiral.

Slowing the breath is the fastest direct intervention available, because the breath is the one part of the autonomic response you can control voluntarily. A slower out-breath activates the parasympathetic nervous system and begins to counteract the adrenaline response. The technique is simple: breathe in for four counts, hold for two, out for six. The longer exhalation is the active part.

Grounding techniques shift attention away from internal sensations and toward the present environment. Touch a surface. Name what you can see and hear. This interrupts the monitoring loop that keeps the misinterpretation cycle running.

Over time, what reduces panic frequency is tolerating the sensations without acting on the interpretation. The more a person sits with the physical experience without catastrophising, the less the alarm system escalates. Avoiding situations that triggered panic tends to keep it going. Returning to those situations gradually, with the skills in place, tends to reduce it.
Draws on David Clark's cognitive model of panic, research on how the fight-or-flight response works in the body, and research on what helps manage panic. Framing is original to homemade.education.
